---
title: "GP1: Omnichannel Journey Map"
subtitle: "CPP Farm Store — Customer Experience Strategy"
author: "Jake Evans, [Name 2], [Name 3], [Name 4], [Name 5]"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
    toc-expand: 2
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    embed-resources: true
    theme: sandstone
    highlight-style: github
editor: visual
execute:
  freeze: auto
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## Executive Summary

This report maps the current and proposed customer journey for the CPP Farm Store at Kellogg Ranch, Cal Poly Pomona. Using a combination of on-site field visits, competitive analysis, and review of prior DMC project work, we identify the key friction points that prevent a seamless omnichannel experience and translate each into concrete opportunities that will drive the rest of this project — our Shopify prototype, retail media setup, campaign plan, creative assets, and analytics dashboard.

::: callout-important
## Key Question

**How can CPP Farm Store create a smoother, more connected customer journey across offline and online channels?**

The store already has a genuinely unique product offering — campus-grown produce, exclusive campus-made goods, and a community-rooted identity that no competitor can replicate. The gap is not product; it is *discoverability, digital infrastructure, and post-visit retention*. This journey map translates that gap into an actionable omnichannel strategy.
:::

## Brand Foundation

Before mapping the journey, it is important to establish what the CPP Farm Store stands for and how that shapes every channel decision.

### Brand Promise

The CPP Farm Store is a farm-fresh retail outlet for Cal Poly Pomona-grown produce, campus-made specialty items, artisanal pantry staples, and seasonal goods. Located at Kellogg Ranch, the store carries products that are genuinely exclusive — campus-pressed wine, CPP craft beer, fresh-ground nut butters, seasonal citrus and avocados sourced directly from the university farm — alongside curated third-party artisanal brands in cheese, deli, and fermented foods.

### Voice and Aesthetic

The store's atmosphere is rustic, mission-focused, and deeply community-oriented. Field visit observations confirmed an environment that feels more like a welcoming farm stand than a retail chain: handwritten signage, a mural in progress, student and staff-run operations, and a manager who describes the store as *"the heart and treasure of campus."*[^1]

[^1]: Direct quote from the CPP Farm Store manager, observed during field visit conducted by Jake Evans, Spring 2026.

### Omnichannel Implication

Digital channels should feel like a natural extension of that in-store warmth — clear, useful, product-led, and local. Large product photography, simple calls-to-action, seasonal collections, and prominent pickup messaging should anchor every digital touchpoint.

## Target Customer Segments {#sec-segments}

Rather than attempting to serve every possible customer type equally, this journey map focuses on one primary segment with three defined micro-personas that reflect meaningful variations in motivation and channel behavior.

### Primary Persona: The CPP Student

::: callout-note
## Emily Rodriguez — Primary Persona

**22 · CPP Student · Mobile-first · Budget-conscious · Between-class shopper**

Emily is the highest-frequency potential customer. She walks past campus touchpoints daily and is reachable via Instagram, Google Maps, and campus signage — but she often does not know the Farm Store exists, cannot quickly find what is in stock, and has no clear digital path to plan or complete a purchase before or during her visit.

**Core need:** A fast, convenient way to discover what is fresh, affordable, seasonal, and available for pickup.

**Purchase triggers:** Instagram Reel, friend recommendation, seasonal campaign, student discount, Google Maps result.

**Success moment:** *"I can plan my visit or order quickly, pick up without friction, and get reminded when seasonal items return."*
:::

### Secondary Micro-Personas

The following three micro-personas share the same core journey structure but differ in motivation, channel preference, and what they need from each touchpoint.

::: panel-tabset
#### A — Campus Convenience Seeker

**Student everyday buyer**

-   **Cue:** Backpack between classes; mobile-first errands; quick lunch or grocery runs.
-   **Hook:** Fresh grab-and-go items, student discounts, campus pickup windows, seasonal favorites.
-   **Message:** *Fresh campus staples between classes.*
-   **Channels:** Instagram Stories/Reels, Google Maps, Shopify pickup, campus signage.
-   **Must-haves:** Accurate hours, live product availability, mobile-friendly pages, simple pickup flow.

#### B — Proud CPP Gifter

**Alumni, parents, and campus visitors**

-   **Cue:** Campus visits, graduation season, alumni pride, holiday gifting, thank-you baskets.
-   **Hook:** CPP-branded gift boxes, local honey, campus wine, jams, and souvenir-quality packaging.
-   **Message:** *Take home a piece of Cal Poly Pomona.*
-   **Channels:** Google Search, gift landing page, email campaigns, in-store displays, campus events.
-   **Must-haves:** Gift bundle options with clear pricing, pickup and shipping clarity, occasion-based categories.

#### C — Local Food Supporter

**Pomona community shopper**

-   **Cue:** Weekend shopping, farmers-market mindset, supporting local growers and students.
-   **Hook:** Seasonal produce, student-grown specialty items, local artisanal goods.
-   **Message:** *Fresh, local, and community-rooted.*
-   **Channels:** Google Maps, SEO, Facebook, neighborhood word-of-mouth, email newsletter.
-   **Must-haves:** Current inventory, seasonal availability calendar, parking and location details, recipe content.
:::

::: callout-note
## Strategic Implication

One core student journey can flex into targeted campaigns for convenience, gifting, and local-community shopping — without requiring separate infrastructure. A well-built Shopify store, a strong Google Business Profile, and a consistent Instagram presence serve all three simultaneously.
:::

## Omnichannel Ecosystem

The following diagram describes the five-channel ecosystem that connects the CPP Farm Store's physical presence to its digital touchpoints across the full customer lifecycle.

| Stage | Primary Channels |
|---|---|
| **Discovery** | Google Search, Google Maps, Instagram, campus signage, word of mouth |
| **Evaluation** | Website / Shopify store, product pages, seasonal content, reviews |
| **Transaction** | Online cart, in-store POS, mobile checkout, student discounts |
| **Fulfillment** | Store visit, BOPIS pickup counter, staff support, packaging |
| **Retention** | Email automation, SMS, loyalty rewards, events, UGC and reviews |

: CPP Farm Store Omnichannel Channel Map {#tbl-channels tbl-colwidths="[25,75]"}

> *Omnichannel principle: each channel should reduce friction for the next step rather than operating as a separate experience. A student who discovers the store on Instagram should be able to check inventory on Shopify, place a pickup order, and receive a confirmation text — all without a phone call.*

## Customer Journey Map {#sec-journey}

### Stage-by-Stage Breakdown

The following section walks through each stage of the student-first journey, documenting what the customer is **doing**, **thinking**, **feeling**, and **needing** — alongside the touchpoints they encounter and the friction they experience.

::: panel-tabset
#### Awareness

**What the customer does:** Sees an Instagram Reel, spots campus signage near Kellogg Ranch, hears about the store from a friend, or stumbles across a Google Maps result while searching for food near campus.

**What the customer thinks:** *"I didn't know CPP had a farm store."*

**What the customer feels:** Curious, mildly surprised — but not yet motivated to act because the digital presence does not reinforce the discovery.

**Friction:** Low organic awareness. The store's Google Business Profile exists but is under-optimized. Instagram content is inconsistent. There is no paid discovery channel. Campus signage is limited to the immediate Kellogg Ranch area, which most students do not pass through regularly.

**Touchpoints:** Instagram, Google Search, Google Maps, word of mouth, campus signage.

**Opportunity:** Strengthen Google Business Profile with updated hours, photos, and product categories. Launch a consistent Instagram Reels content calendar anchored to seasonal drops and campus-exclusive products. Invest in Google Search and Maps visibility as the primary paid discovery lever.

#### Consideration

**What the customer does:** Searches for the Farm Store online, visits the current website (`cpp.edu/farmstore`), looks for store hours, checks what products are available, and tries to determine whether the trip is worth it.

**What the customer thinks:** *"Do they have what I need? Are they even open right now?"*

**What the customer feels:** Interested but uncertain — the current website does not surface product inventory clearly, and hours are not immediately prominent.

**Friction:** Field visit observations confirmed that the current website does not function as a browsable product catalog. Product lines exist (campus produce, campus-made specialty items, artisanal cheese, deli, pickles, gift baskets) but are not organized in a way that lets a mobile user quickly identify what is in stock today. There is no seasonal availability calendar and no product photography at the inventory level.

**Touchpoints:** `cpp.edu/farmstore`, Google Maps listing, Instagram profile.

**Opportunity:** Build a Shopify product catalog organized around the six observed product lines, with seasonal collections that are updated regularly. Prioritize campus-exclusive SKUs — the CP Horseshoe Vineyard wines, CPP craft beer, dragon fruit, guava, and fresh-ground nut butters — because these are the items no competitor carries at any price point and represent the strongest conversion argument.

#### Purchase

**What the customer does:** Attempts to place an order online, or decides to visit in person without a clear sense of what to expect at checkout.

**What the customer thinks:** *"Can I actually buy this online? Or do I have to call?"*

**What the customer feels:** Frustrated. Field visit confirmed that the current ordering process is entirely manual — orders are taken by phone or email, payments are completed via Zelle or over the phone, and gift baskets require 24–48 hours advance notice because they are custom-assembled.

**Friction:** No e-commerce checkout. No Apple Pay or Google Pay at point of sale. No BOPIS (buy online, pick up in store) workflow. Competitors — Whole Foods, Sprouts, and to a lesser extent Trader Joe's — all offer digital checkout infrastructure that the Farm Store currently lacks.

**Touchpoints:** Phone call, email, in-store POS, word of mouth.

**Opportunity:** Implement a Shopify-powered BOPIS workflow. Build a gift bundle pre-ordering system so the 24–48 hour lead time becomes a feature (custom-assembled to order) rather than a hidden friction point. Enable mobile-friendly checkout as the primary path for the student segment.

#### Pickup

**What the customer does:** Arrives at the Kellogg Ranch location to collect an order or make an in-store purchase.

**What the customer thinks:** *"Where do I go? Is my order ready?"*

**What the customer feels:** Satisfied with the in-store experience once they arrive — field visit confirmed the service is warm, personal, and community-driven — but the journey *to* that moment is unclear for a first-time visitor.

**Friction:** No digital pickup confirmation. No SMS notification system. Parking and wayfinding to the Kellogg Ranch location are not well-communicated through any digital channel. First-time visitors navigating from Google Maps may find the entrance confusing.

**Touchpoints:** Physical store, staff, parking, campus wayfinding.

**Opportunity:** Add pickup confirmation SMS to the Shopify order workflow. Create a dedicated "Find Us / Pickup Info" page with parking instructions, a campus map embed, and expected pickup windows. Feature this prominently on the Shopify homepage and in the post-purchase email.

#### Post-Purchase

**What the customer does:** Uses the product, potentially shares content on social media, and either returns or does not.

**What the customer thinks:** *"That was worth it"* — but there is no follow-up communication to reinforce that feeling or prompt a return.

**What the customer feels:** Happy with the product, but disconnected from the store. There is no digital relationship after the transaction ends.

**Friction:** No post-purchase email sequence. No review prompt. No recipe content or product use inspiration. No mechanism to announce seasonal restocks to past customers.

**Touchpoints:** Product packaging, personal use, social sharing (informal).

**Opportunity:** Launch a post-purchase email sequence: (1) order confirmation, (2) recipe or usage inspiration tied to the purchased product, (3) seasonal restock alert, (4) review request. Use product packaging as a QR code touchpoint that drives to the Shopify store and email signup.

#### Loyalty

**What the customer does:** Returns for seasonal items, recommends the store to friends, or purchases a gift basket for a campus occasion.

**What the customer thinks:** *"This is my campus store."*

**What the customer feels:** Connected to CPP identity and proud to support a campus institution — but only if the store has maintained a relationship through the channels above.

**Friction:** No loyalty program. No referral mechanism. No structured way to communicate seasonal drops, limited-run products, or campus events to returning customers.

**Touchpoints:** Email, Instagram, word of mouth, campus events.

**Opportunity:** Implement a simple loyalty program through Shopify (points per purchase, redeemable on future orders). Create a seasonal email calendar tied to the Farm Store's natural inventory cycles — citrus season, avocado season, holiday gift baskets, campus wine releases. Build a referral incentive for students who bring new customers to the store.
:::

## Pain Points {#sec-pain}

The following pain points were identified through field visit observations, review of the current website and social media presence, and competitive benchmarking against Trader Joe's, Whole Foods Market, and Sprouts Farmers Market in the Brea/Pomona area.

| Pain Point | Evidence | Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Low digital discoverability** | Google Business Profile exists but is under-optimized; Instagram content is inconsistent | Students do not know the store exists |
| **No browsable online catalog** | `cpp.edu/farmstore` does not display product inventory; no product photography at the item level | Consideration stage fails before a visit is attempted |
| **Fully manual ordering** | Orders taken by phone/email; no Apple Pay or Google Pay at POS; gift baskets require 24–48 hr notice | High friction purchase stage; lost impulse buyers |
| **No BOPIS or mobile checkout** | All competitors offer digital checkout infrastructure; Farm Store does not | Students with 15 minutes between classes cannot transact |
| **Below-market pricing on exclusives** | Manager acknowledged pricing gap; campus wine, beer, and nut butters are underpriced relative to their scarcity | Revenue left on the table; exclusivity not communicated |
| **No post-purchase communication** | No email sequence, no review prompt, no restock notification | Retention and repeat purchase rate unknown and likely low |
| **No loyalty or referral mechanism** | No points program; no structured referral incentive | Word-of-mouth growth is entirely passive |
| **Pickup friction for first-time visitors** | Parking and wayfinding to Kellogg Ranch not well-communicated digitally | First visit experience is needlessly stressful |

: Identified Pain Points with Evidence {#tbl-pain tbl-colwidths="[25,40,35]"}

::: callout-important
## Highest-Impact Pain Points

Two pain points stand out as the most structurally significant: **no browsable online catalog** and **fully manual ordering**. These two gaps mean that the entire consideration and purchase stages of the journey currently require a phone call — an experience that is incompatible with the mobile-first behavior of the primary student segment. Every other opportunity in this strategy depends on solving these two first.
:::

## Omnichannel Opportunities {#sec-opps}

For each pain point identified above, the following opportunities translate friction into action. These opportunities are sequenced into three implementation phases and directly correspond to the deliverables that follow in the rest of the project.

### Phase 1 — Foundation: Fix Discovery and Trust Signals

These are the zero-cost or low-cost improvements that remove the biggest barriers to awareness and consideration.

-   **Optimize the Google Business Profile** with accurate hours, product categories, seasonal photos, and a direct link to the Shopify store. This is the single highest-leverage action for organic discovery.
-   **Standardize Instagram content** around a weekly posting cadence. Seasonal produce drops, campus-exclusive product reveals, and behind-the-scenes farm and store content are the three content pillars with the highest organic reach potential.
-   **Add campus signage** near high-traffic student areas (the University Village, the CLA building, the Recreation Center) that directs to the Farm Store Instagram and Google Maps listing with a QR code.

### Phase 2 — Commerce: Build a Shoppable Digital Layer

These improvements require the Shopify build and represent the core of the GP2 deliverable.

-   **Build a Shopify product catalog** organized around the six observed product lines: campus-grown produce, campus-made specialty items, artisanal cheese and dips, deli and charcuterie, pickles and fermented foods, and gift baskets. Campus-exclusive SKUs should anchor the homepage and seasonal collection pages.
-   **Implement BOPIS** as the primary checkout path for the student segment. The 24–48 hour lead time for gift baskets becomes a value-add (custom-assembled to order) when communicated proactively through the checkout flow.
-   **Enable mobile-friendly checkout** with Apple Pay and Google Pay as default payment options.
-   **Create a gift bundle landing page** organized by occasion (graduation, alumni gifting, holiday, thank-you) to serve the Gifter persona and capture higher average order values.

### Phase 3 — Activation: Launch Campaigns and Retention Loops

These improvements represent the GP3–GP5 deliverables: retail media, campaign creative, and dashboard.

-   **Launch Google Shopping and Search campaigns** anchored to the campus-exclusive product catalog. Campus-made wine, CPP craft beer, and seasonal citrus and avocado are the hero products — none of the three competitors carry them, and Google Shopping allows the Farm Store to surface them at the exact moment a consumer is searching.
-   **Build a post-purchase email sequence** (confirmation → inspiration → restock alert → review request) in Shopify Email or a connected ESP.
-   **Implement a simple loyalty program** through Shopify's native points infrastructure.
-   **Build a KPI dashboard** that maps journey-stage metrics to business outcomes: discovery (Google impressions, Maps views), evaluation (product page views, collection engagement), conversion (add-to-cart rate, checkout completion), fulfillment (BOPIS orders, pickup satisfaction), and retention (email open rate, repeat purchase rate, loyalty signups).

::: callout-note
## Strategic Implication for the Rest of the Project

This journey map is the strategic brief for all subsequent group deliverables. The Shopify prototype (GP2) should address the consideration and purchase gaps. The retail media setup (GP3) should address the discovery gap. The campaign plan and creative assets (GP4) should address the awareness and retention gaps. The dashboard (GP5) should make all of these measurable. Nothing in the remaining deliverables should be built without a clear line back to a pain point or opportunity identified here.
:::

## Competitive Context {#sec-competitive}

Field visit observations and competitive analysis of Trader Joe's (Diamond Bar), Whole Foods Market (Brea), and Sprouts Farmers Market (Brea) informed the pain point and opportunity identification above. The following summary places the Farm Store's omnichannel gaps in competitive context.

### What the Competition Does That the Farm Store Currently Does Not

-   **E-commerce platforms** (Whole Foods via Amazon, Sprouts via Instacart, Trader Joe's via limited web presence)
-   **Loyalty apps** (Whole Foods Prime, Sprouts loyalty program)
-   **Digital checkout** with Apple Pay and Google Pay at POS
-   **Inventory visibility** through online product listings

### What the Farm Store Has That the Competition Cannot Replicate

-   Campus-grown produce sourced directly from the CPP farm — *no competitor, at any price point, can source from a university farm*
-   Campus-made exclusive products: CP Horseshoe Vineyard wines, CPP craft beer, fresh-ground nut butters, wildflower honey
-   A community and educational identity that is genuinely mission-driven
-   Experiential offerings (farm tours, planned scavenger hunts, citrus-to-juice programming) that no grocery competitor offers

::: callout-note
## The Competitive Insight

The CPP Farm Store does not need to out-spend Whole Foods on digital marketing. It needs to make its genuinely unique products *findable*. A student or alumni searching Google for "CPP avocados" or "Cal Poly wine" should immediately find a product page they can purchase from. That is the entire digital strategy in one sentence.
:::

## Measurement Framework

The following KPIs correspond to each stage of the customer journey and will form the basis of the GP5 analytics dashboard.

| Journey Stage | Metric | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Awareness** | Google impressions, Maps views, profile clicks | Indicates whether discovery channels are working |
| **Consideration** | Product page views, collection views, IG link clicks | Indicates whether the catalog is compelling |
| **Purchase** | Add-to-cart rate, checkout starts, conversion rate | Indicates whether the transaction path is functional |
| **Fulfillment** | BOPIS orders placed, pickup completions | Indicates adoption of the new commerce infrastructure |
| **Retention** | Email open rate, repeat purchase rate, loyalty signups | Indicates whether the post-purchase relationship is working |

: GP5 Dashboard KPI Framework {#tbl-kpi tbl-colwidths="[20,40,40]"}

## Appendix {.unnumbered}

### Grading Criteria Self-Assessment {.unnumbered}

| Criterion | Points Possible | Where Addressed in This Report |
|---|---|---|
| Clear target customer segment | 15 | @sec-segments |
| Complete journey stages | 15 | @sec-journey |
| Strong identification of touchpoints | 15 | @sec-journey (each stage) |
| Insightful pain points | 20 | @sec-pain |
| Practical omnichannel opportunities | 20 | @sec-opps |
| Visual clarity and professionalism | 15 | Tables, callouts, panel tabsets throughout |
| **Total** | **100** | |

: Rubric Self-Assessment {#tbl-rubric tbl-colwidths="[35,20,45]"}

### Project Links {.unnumbered}

::: callout-note
## Group 6 Project Links

-   **GitHub Repository:** [https://github.com/jakevns/RStudio](https://github.com/jakevns/RStudio)
-   **GitHub Pages (Live Report):** [https://jakevns.github.io/RStudio/](https://jakevns.github.io/RStudio/)

*Update these links once the GP1 folder is published to GitHub Pages.*
:::

### Field Visit Reference {.unnumbered}

Field visit observations were conducted by Jake Evans, Spring 2026, and documented in a separate competitive analysis report covering product assortment, pricing strategy, service model, store atmosphere, and perceptual mapping against Trader Joe's, Whole Foods Market, and Sprouts Farmers Market.
